skill.name: vercel-react-native-skills
skill.description: React Native and Expo guidance for building faster mobile apps with safer rendering, better lists, smoother animations, native navigation, and platform-aware UI patterns.
skill.summary: Apply React Native and Expo performance best practices with focused rules and examples.
skill.author: vercel-labs

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows they are markdown examples, documentation links, frontmatter, and React Native terminology. No executable scripts, malicious network behavior, credential access, prompt injection, or data exfiltration intent was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick False Positives
low_findings.description: Static findings labeled many markdown backticks and TypeScript examples as shell or Ruby command execution. The cited files are documentation and rule examples; they do not define executable scripts or run commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The same pattern appears inside markdown code fences and inline code references. No child process API, shell invocation, or install hook is present in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: Documentation URL False Positives
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL findings point to public documentation references for React, React Native, Expo, Reanimated, LegendList, Zeego, and related libraries. These links are reference material, not outbound requests made by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are listed as documentation references and are not used by runtime code. I found no fetch, curl, webhook, or telemetry behavior tied to these links.

low_findings.title: Keylogger Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: Keylogger detections are caused by React Native performance terms such as keystroke, keyword, keyExtractor, and recyclingKey. The content discusses list rendering and memoization, not input capture or surveillance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text is about reducing list re-renders during typing and building domain strings. There is no evidence of keyboard event capture, persistence, or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Key File False Positives
low_findings.description: Certificate and key-file alerts map to React list key concepts and markdown file names, not private keys or certificates. No PEM blocks, private-key material, or certificate files were found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited terminology refers to component keys, cache policies, and markdown naming conventions. I did not find secret material or instructions to read secret files.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto False Positives
low_findings.description: Weak cryptographic algorithm alerts appear on metadata, impact labels, and markdown section descriptions. The skill does not implement cryptography or recommend MD5, SHA-1, DES, or other weak algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are descriptive prose and frontmatter, not crypto code. No evidence found for hashing, encryption, password storage, or cryptographic API usage.

content.value_statement: React Native apps often lose performance through unstable lists, heavy components, and JS-driven UI patterns. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code concrete rules for faster mobile code.
actual_capabilities: Guides React Native rendering choices that prevent runtime crashes.
Recommends virtualized list patterns with stable references and lightweight rows.
Explains Reanimated and gesture patterns for smoother UI-thread animations.
Covers native navigation, safe areas, modals, menus, images, and font loading.
Provides monorepo and dependency guidance for Expo and native modules.
Supplies incorrect and correct examples for each documented rule.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not run automated code transformations.
It assumes a React Native or Expo project context.
Some recommendations depend on project dependencies such as Reanimated or Expo Image.
Teams should validate library choices against their supported platform versions.

use_cases.title: Improve Feed Performance
use_cases.description: Use the list rules to replace eager rendering, stabilize row props, and reduce scroll jank in feeds or search results.

use_cases.title: Review Animation Patterns
use_cases.description: Check whether animations use transform, opacity, shared values, and gesture handlers instead of layout updates or JS-thread press states.

use_cases.title: Standardize App Architecture
use_cases.description: Create team guidance for navigation, image handling, native dependencies, fonts, and design system imports across React Native apps.

prompt_templates.title: Check A Component
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one React Native component
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this React Native component using the React Native Skills rules. Focus on rendering safety, basic performance, and clear fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize A List Screen
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this list screen for virtualizer use, stable data references, row memoization, callbacks, images, and item type handling.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Animation Responsiveness
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these animations and press states. Recommend Reanimated, shared values, transform or opacity changes, and GestureDetector patterns where appropriate.

prompt_templates.title: Create Team Guidelines
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the React Native Skills rules to produce a project-specific checklist for navigation, UI components, state, monorepo dependencies, images, and fonts.

output_examples.input: A feed screen maps items inside a scroll view and passes inline callbacks to every row.
output: The review recommends replacing eager rendering with a virtualized list.
It flags unstable callbacks and object props as causes of extra row renders.
It suggests moving heavy work and image resizing outside visible row rendering.

output_examples.input: A button animation uses press callbacks on the JS thread and changes layout properties.
output: The review recommends using gesture handling with UI-thread shared values.
It suggests animating transform and opacity instead of width, height, margin, or padding.
It explains which parts should remain regular Pressable behavior if no animation is needed.

output_examples.input: A team wants React Native standards for a new Expo app.
output: The output organizes standards by list performance, rendering, navigation, UI, state, monorepo setup, and fonts.
It calls out dependency assumptions and areas that require platform testing.

best_practices: Start with crash-prevention and list performance rules before smaller optimizations.
Ask for project context, dependency versions, and platform targets before applying library-specific advice.
Use the incorrect and correct examples as review anchors, then adapt fixes to local conventions.
anti_patterns: Do not apply every rule mechanically without checking the project architecture.
Do not replace working native navigation or image libraries without migration planning.
Do not treat documentation examples as complete production code.

faq.question: Does this skill edit React Native code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides review guidance and examples that an AI assistant can apply during coding tasks.

faq.question: Which projects benefit most from this skill?
faq.answer: React Native and Expo apps with lists, animations, native navigation, images, or shared mobile UI patterns benefit most.

faq.question: Does it require Expo?
faq.answer: No. Many rules apply to React Native directly, but some recommendations mention Expo-specific libraries and configuration.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this skill for reviews?
faq.answer: Yes. The rule files provide concrete checks that Claude Code, Codex, and Claude can use during code review or implementation.

faq.question: Is the guidance focused on security?
faq.answer: No. The primary focus is performance, rendering safety, native behavior, and maintainable React Native architecture.

faq.question: How should teams adopt the rules?
faq.answer: Start with the rules that match current pain points, then turn repeated findings into project-specific lint rules or review checklists.
